details.text: Derivative: يَدَا literally means “two hands”. It can refer to his two hands literally in the case that they were used to harm the Messenger ﷺ. It can also refer to his actions or all that he does in the case where the hands are most responsible for human actions. The hands can also refer to him by using a part to describe the whole. The meaning of the verse can therefore be “May Abu Lahab and all that he does be destroyed”.

Derivative: تَبَّتْ is in the feminine form of the word تَبَّ – تَبٌّ. It is in the past tense and means to be destroyed, to perish or to lose and be ruined in a manner that leads to destruction or perishing. It can also mean to break apart bit by bit i.e., a deterioration to the point of destruction or a slow and steady destruction leading to someone’s or something’s demise. It was used by the Arabs to describe becoming old and decrepit. The Arabs did not only use the past tense form to refer to the past but also used it to imply the meaning of the future. The past tense is associated with certainty and as such it is used to imply that a matter is guaranteed to happen. The meaning in the context of this verse would therefore be: “The hands of Abu Lahab are guaranteed to be destroyed”. The past tense is also used when a supplication is intended whether that supplication is for or against somebody. It is like the supplication جَزاك اللهُ خَيْرًا (May Allah reward you with good) or the supplication شَفَاكَ الله (May Allah cure you). Although the past tense is used the meaning is connected to the future to mean in this Verse: “May the hands of Abu Lahab be destroyed”.
Derivative: لَهَبٍ (Lahab) is a flame from a fire and has been described as a red flame with flickering in it. It has been described to resemble a snake’s tongue.

تَبَّتْ يَدَا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ

- Concerning the repeated use of the verb تَبَّ (Tabba - to be destroyed) at the end of the verse after being used in the feminine form تَبَّتْ (Tabbat) at the beginning of the verse. The first is said to refer to a supplication against him whilst the second usage indicates the realisation of that supplication i.e., May he be destroyed and indeed he has been destroyed.

Derivative: أَغْنَىٰ with عَنْ carries the meaning of to benefit and avail. مَا أَغْنَىٰ عَنْ implies the meaning of “It will not benefit or avail at all”. أَغْنَىٰ is the past tense which is being used to refer to the future in the case where the use of the past tense in reference to the future is used to imply certainty due to the certainty intrinsic to the past tense.
Derivative: مَا (Maa) is a more emphatic negation than لَمْ (Lam). مَا is used to negate a countering or opposing idea. It can be understood with the meaning of “not at all”.
Derivative: كَسَبَ means to earn and attain. It can include property, money and children.

مَا أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُ مَالُهُ وَمَا كَسَبَ

- The third person is used in respect to Abu Lahab which fits the Quranic pattern of not using the first person to address the worst kinds of disbelievers, whom Allah does not speak directly to in this life and the next.
Derivative: سَ is used to imply the near future implying that the verb it is attached to will be realised very soon. The سَ also implies a threat and promise of the verb’s occurrence.

Derivative: امْرَأَة in origin means “a woman” but when it is used in the إّضافَة (Idaafah) construct when adding the woman to someone (i.e., the woman of someone) the meaning of woman changes to wife. اِمْرَأَة (Imra’ah) can also mean wife if the context dictates that meaning. It has also been said that the word اِمْرَأَة (Imra’ah) is used to refer to the wife instead of the word زَوْج (Zawj) when the wife is missing something, like harmony in the marriage or a child.

Derivative: حَمَّالَةَ means someone who carries something or a carrier. It is in the hyperbolised form and indicates to someone who keeps on carrying i.e., she will not just carry but rather keep carrying). It is in the نَصْب (accusative) state or case with a فَتْحَة (Fat’hah - َ) on its final letter because it is مَفْعُول بِهِ (Maf’ool Bihi - The object of a verb) in the case where the verb has been omitted. The verb that is omitted is linked with condemnation here (الذَمّ – Adh-Dhamm). The anger and disgust directed towards her is therefore captured in this فَتْحَة (Fat’hah – َ).
Derivative: الْحَطَبِ is the dry wood that is collected and used to make fire for heating.
“And his wife” follows on from the previous verse in the Surah meaning: “Abu Lahab will enter to burn in the fire of flames and his wife will also enter with him”.

Derivative: حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ “The carrier of firwood” was a phrase used to describe the salves that would gather and carry firewood for their masters. Its usage takes on greater meaning in the context of someone who used to revel in their status being told that they will carry the title in the next life of those who used to be their slaves in this life. الْحَطَبِ “The carrier of firewood” was also an Arab expression for someone who carries speech around which causes fires (i.e., discord) to erupt amongst people”. According to this meaning it could be a description of her in the life of this world rather than the hereafter.
Derivative: مَسَد (Masad) is (palm) fibre that is twisted one over another and entwined in a perfect manner. It is also used for a material that is not easy to burn.
Derivative: جِيدِ refers to the neck of a woman and the place of a necklace.
